text.title: Gcp Networking Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules for the gcp-networking methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale + concrete remediation hook.

rule.statement: Multi-project environments inside one organisation MUST use a Shared VPC host project for shared networking — never project-per-VPC mesh.
rule.rationale: Shared VPC centralises networking ops on a single host project; per-project meshes explode firewall and peering complexity.

rule.statement: Private GCE / GKE workloads needing internet egress MUST use Cloud NAT; per-instance public IPs are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Per-instance public IPs widen attack surface and waste IP quota; Cloud NAT is the supported pattern.

rule.statement: Public HTTP(S) endpoints MUST sit behind a Global External HTTPS Load Balancer with Cloud Armor enabled and Cloud CDN where appropriate.
rule.rationale: Global HTTPS LB delivers consistent edge termination, WAF (Cloud Armor), and CDN — the canonical edge for GCP.

rule.statement: Access to GCP-managed services (Cloud SQL, Memorystore, GKE control plane) from VPC MUST use Private Service Connect or PSA, not public IPs.
rule.rationale: PSC keeps traffic inside Google's network and eliminates public-endpoint exposure.

rule.statement: Production VPCs MUST enable VPC Flow Logs (with sampling tuned for cost) and ship them to Cloud Logging.
rule.rationale: Flow logs are the only retro-active source of truth during a security or networking incident.

rule.statement: If the input fails ALL "Applies If" preconditions or matches ANY "Skip If" disqualifier, do not apply this methodology — route the task to the adjacent sibling instead.
rule.rationale: Even flat checklists need an explicit skip leaf in the decision tree; this rule is the universal terminal.
